summary: 5a resolves Reish Lakish's challenge from 3b: if we derive YK from the inauguration, all inauguration requirements should invalidate the YK service. Four Amoraim name practical differences between R. Yochanan (who holds that all inauguration requirements invalidate) and R. Chanina (who holds only those that would invalidate ongoing service also invalidate the inauguration). Rav Yosef names semikha (laying hands on the offering); Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak names tenufah (waving); Rav Pappa names prisha (seven-day sequestering). In the second sugya, Ravina adds rivui shiva (wearing multiple garments for seven days) and meshicha shiva (anointing for seven days). The Gemara then provides sources from Lev. 16:32 for why any combination of anointing and garment-wearing over seven days suffices. R. Yitzchak bar Bisna identifies the word 'kachah' (thus/so) as the source for R. Yochanan's invalidation principle, and the daf ends with a stub question: where do we derive that inauguration requirements not explicitly written in the inauguration passage but written elsewhere also invalidate?

display.title: R. Yochanan vs. R. Chanina: semikha, tenufah, and prisha as practical differences
display.oneLine: Three authorities list practical differences between R. Yochanan (all inauguration requirements invalidate) and R. Chanina (only those that invalidate ongoing service invalidate the inauguration): semikha, tenufah, and prisha each do not invalidate ongoing service and thus diverge.
display.shortSummary: Rav Yosef identifies semikha (laying hands on the offering) as a point of divergence: R. Yochanan would say failure to perform semikha at the inauguration invalidates, while R. Chanina says it does not, because semikha does not invalidate ongoing service. A baraita proves semikha does not invalidate ongoing service: Lev. 1:4 says laying hands causes the offering 'to be accepted' - but does waving hands atone? No, only blood atones. The verse must be teaching that if you treat semikha as optional (a 'shirei mitzva'), the Torah credits you as if you did not achieve full atonement - even though you did atone. The same structure applies to tenufah (Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak). For prisha, Rav Pappa notes that the mishna uses 'matkin' (designate) not 'mafrishin' (separate), proving the backup priest does not need to be sequestered - which means sequestering does not invalidate ongoing service.
display.whats: The Gemara now resolves Reish Lakish's challenge from 3b. R. Yochanan held that all inauguration requirements invalidate because of the word 'kachah' - do exactly as written. R. Chanina had a narrower principle: only those requirements that would invalidate ordinary ongoing service also invalidate the inauguration. Three authorities name items where these views diverge. Rav Yosef: semikha. At the inauguration, the Kohen laid hands on the offering (a requirement written there). Does failure to do semikha invalidate? R. Yochanan says yes. R. Chanina says no - because semikha does not invalidate ongoing sacrificial service. Proof from Lev. 1:4: 'he shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering and it shall be accepted for him to atone' - does hand-laying atone? Only blood atones (Lev. 17:11)! So why say 'to atone'? Because if a person omits semikha, treating it as a secondary mitzva (shirei mitzva), the Torah considers it as if he did not achieve the ideal atonement, even though he still atones. This proves semikha is a shirei mitzva, not indispensable. Tenufah (Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak) follows the same pattern from its verse. Prisha (Rav Pappa): the mishna says 'matkin' (they prepare a backup) not 'mafrishin' (they also separate the backup), showing sequestering is not indispensable for ongoing service.
display.hint: The concept of 'shirei mitzva' (residual mitzva) is key: why does the Torah say 'to atone' in connection with an act that does not actually accomplish atonement? What does this category reveal about how mitzvot work?

learning.learnerQuestion: If semikha doesn't actually atone - only blood atones - why does the verse say 'it shall be accepted for him to atone' when describing semikha?
learning.coreTension: The verse seems to say hand-laying causes atonement. But the Gemara knows from another verse that only blood causes atonement. Something has to give - either the first verse is wrong, or it means something else.
learning.coreMove: Introduce the category of 'shirei mitzva': a residual, secondary component of a mitzva that is not itself the atonement mechanism but whose omission is still counted against you. The verse's 'to atone' is conditional, not causal: it signals that neglecting this optional step means the Torah credits you with less than optimal atonement.
learning.resolution: The verse teaches: if you treat semikha as optional and skip it, the Torah considers this as if your atonement were incomplete - even though you did atone through the blood. This proves semikha is a shirei mitzva (not indispensable), and therefore its omission does not invalidate ongoing service. Consequently (for R. Chanina), it also does not invalidate the inauguration service.

takeaway.text: The category of 'shirei mitzva' captures optional mitzva components: they do not validate or invalidate the core transaction, but their omission still registers as a spiritual deficit. Recognizing this category allows the Gemara to read verses about secondary actions without over-inflating their legal weight.

reasoningPattern.notes: When a verse attributes a function to an act that cannot literally perform that function, the Gemara introduces 'shirei mitzva': the act is secondary and optional, and its omission is scored as incompleteness without being an invalidation.

learning.ahaMoment: The verse says semikha causes 'acceptance' and 'atonement' - but another verse already established that only blood atones. The resolution is not to say one verse overrides the other, but to recognize a different category: optional, residual components of a mitzva that affect how fully you have fulfilled the commandment without being the core mechanism.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think 'shirei mitzva' means an unimportant mitzva. The Gemara's point is more precise: it is a mitzva that is real and matters spiritually, but is not an invalidation condition. Skipping it doesn't void the service, but it does leave something incomplete.
learning.memoryAnchor: Semikha, tenufah, prisha: three inauguration requirements that don't invalidate ongoing service. The verse that 'assigns atonement' to hand-laying actually teaches you're spiritually docked for skipping it - but the offering is still valid.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Yosef: semikha diverges
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Yosef
argumentFlow.text: Semikha is a practical difference: R. Yochanan holds that failing to perform semikha at the inauguration invalidates; R. Chanina holds it does not, because semikha does not invalidate ongoing service.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Yosef names semikha as the first practical difference

argumentFlow.label: Semikha does not invalidate ongoing service: baraita from Lev. 1:4
argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna (baraita)
argumentFlow.text: Lev. 1:4 says 'he shall lay his hand and it shall be accepted for him to atone.' Does hand-laying atone? Only blood atones (Lev. 17:11)! So 'to atone' here means: if you treat semikha as optional, the Torah credits you as if you did not achieve ideal atonement - even though you did atone.

sourceRefs.note: Baraita proving semikha is a shirei mitzva, not an invalidation condition

argumentFlow.label: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak: tenufah diverges
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak
argumentFlow.text: Tenufah (waving) is another practical difference, following the same structure: 'to wave it to make atonement' - but waving does not atone. It is a shirei mitzva. Therefore R. Chanina holds omitting it does not invalidate the inauguration.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak names tenufah as the second difference

argumentFlow.label: Tenufah proof: same baraita structure
argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna (baraita)
argumentFlow.text: The tenufah verse says 'to wave to atone' - but waving does not atone. Same reasoning as semikha: treating tenufah as optional means the Torah credits you with incomplete atonement, but you still atoned. Tenufah is a shirei mitzva.

sourceRefs.note: Baraita for tenufah parallel to semikha

argumentFlow.label: Rav Pappa: prisha diverges
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Pappa
argumentFlow.text: Prisha (sequestering) is a third difference. The proof that it does not invalidate ongoing service: the mishna says 'matkin' (they designate a backup priest), not 'mafrishin' (they also separate the backup). If sequestering were indispensable, the backup would also need separation - the word choice proves it is not.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Pappa names prisha; matkin vs. mafrishin proof

argumentFlow.label: Prisha proof: matkin not mafrishin
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The mishna's use of 'matkin' instead of 'mafrishin' for the backup priest proves that the backup serves validly without prior separation. Since sequestering does not invalidate ongoing service, R. Chanina holds it does not invalidate the inauguration either.

conceptRefs.label: Shirei Mitzva - residual optional mitzva component
R. Yochanan vs. R. Chanina: scope of inauguration requirements

misconceptions.misconception: Shirei mitzva means the act is unimportant or spiritually trivial.
misconceptions.correction: A shirei mitzva is a real mitzva obligation whose omission registers as spiritual incompleteness - the Torah still counts it against you. It differs from an indispensable condition in that its omission does not void the service.

misconceptions.misconception: The verse 'it shall be accepted to atone' proves that hand-laying itself accomplishes atonement.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara reads this verse through the lens of Lev. 17:11, which unambiguously limits atonement to blood. The verse about semikha must therefore be understood as a conditional warning: omitting semikha leaves your atonement spiritually incomplete, even though the blood still atones.

visualizableElements.item: Side-by-side table: three inauguration requirements (semikha, tenufah, prisha) with R. Yochanan's ruling (invalidates) vs. R. Chanina's ruling (does not), each with a one-line proof
Shirei mitzva spectrum diagram: on one end 'core atonement mechanism (blood),' on the other 'totally optional action.' Semikha and tenufah sit in the middle - real but not invalidating.

quizSeeds.question: What is the practical halakhic difference between R. Yochanan and R. Chanina regarding inauguration requirements?
quizSeeds.answer: R. Yochanan: any requirement that was written in the inauguration passage invalidates the service if omitted, even if it would not invalidate ongoing service. R. Chanina: only requirements that would invalidate ordinary ongoing service also invalidate the inauguration.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara explain Lev. 1:4's phrase 'to atone' in connection with semikha, if only blood actually atones?
quizSeeds.answer: The Gemara classifies semikha as a 'shirei mitzva.' When the verse says 'to atone,' it does not mean semikha mechanically causes atonement. Rather, if you treat semikha as optional and skip it, the Torah credits you as if your atonement were incomplete - even though the blood did atone. The phrase is a warning about spiritual incompleteness, not a claim about the mechanism.

quizSeeds.question: How does Rav Pappa prove that prisha does not invalidate ongoing sacrificial service?
quizSeeds.answer: From the mishna's word choice: it says 'matkin' (they prepare/designate a backup priest) rather than 'mafrishin' (they also separate the backup). If sequestering were indispensable, the backup would also need formal separation. The fact that the mishna only designates but does not separate the backup proves sequestering is not an invalidation condition.

sugyot.finalRuling: Semikha, tenufah, and prisha are all shirei mitzva - real but optional components of the sacrifice that do not invalidate the service if omitted. R. Chanina therefore holds they do not invalidate the inauguration service either, since only requirements that would invalidate ongoing service can invalidate the inauguration.
alternateAngles: The Gemara's shirei mitzva category reveals a theology of mitzva performance: there is a difference between 'you atoned' (valid service, blood accepted) and 'you atoned optimally' (full performance of all mitzva components). The Torah cares about both, even if only the latter is legally indispensable.

display.title: Ravina adds rivui and meshicha; R. Yitzchak bar Bisna gives the source for R. Yochanan's principle
display.oneLine: Ravina adds wearing priestly garments for seven days (rivui shiva) and anointing for seven days (meshicha shiva) to the list of divergences; a baraita proves any combination of these suffices; R. Yitzchak bar Bisna derives R. Yochanan's invalidation principle from 'kachah'; the daf ends open on where non-inauguration-passage requirements derive from.
display.shortSummary: Ravina identifies two more practical differences: rivui shiva (wearing the full set of priestly garments across seven days) and meshicha shiva (anointing across seven days). A baraita on Lev. 16:32 ('the priest who shall be anointed and who shall be consecrated') proves that any combination of anointing and garment-wearing over seven days suffices. The need for anointing over seven days is derived from a juxtaposition: since garment-wearing requires seven days, and anointing is juxtaposed to garment-wearing, anointing also requires seven days. R. Yitzchak bar Bisna then gives the source for R. Yochanan's broad invalidation principle: the word 'kachah' (thus/so) in 'do thus to Aaron and his sons' means exact replication is required, making all explicitly written inauguration details indispensable. The daf ends with an open question: from where do we derive that inauguration requirements written elsewhere (not in the inauguration passage itself) also invalidate?
display.whats: Ravina adds two more items to the list: rivui shiva (the Kohen Gadol wearing the full priestly garments on each of the seven days) and meshicha shiva (anointing him on each of the seven days). R. Yochanan holds omitting either invalidates; R. Chanina holds neither does, because neither invalidates ongoing service. A baraita on Lev. 16:32 establishes that any combination of anointing and garment-wearing over seven days makes a valid Kohen Gadol: Exod. 29:30 says 'seven days shall the son wear them,' implying that wearing garments for seven days - or anointing for seven days - or both - all suffice. The source that anointing requires seven days ab initio: it is juxtaposed to garment-wearing in a verse ('the sacred garments of Aaron shall be for his sons to be anointed in them and consecrated in them'), and since garment-wearing requires seven days, anointing does too. R. Yitzchak bar Bisna then derives R. Yochanan's principle: the word 'kachah' in 'do thus to Aaron and his sons' means exact replication, so every detail written in the inauguration passage must be observed exactly - and failure to do so invalidates. But 'kachah' only covers things explicitly written in the inauguration context. The final stub question: how do we derive that requirements written elsewhere (outside the inauguration passage) also invalidate? The daf ends here.
display.hint: The word 'kachah' (thus) in a Torah command signals exact replication. What does this imply about inauguration requirements written in other contexts - like the priestly garment rules in Exodus 28?

learning.learnerQuestion: R. Yitzchak bar Bisna derives that 'kachah' makes inauguration requirements obligatory. But that only applies to things written in the inauguration passage itself. How do we know that requirements written elsewhere also apply?
learning.coreTension: The word 'kachah' is in the inauguration passage, so it can only make mandatory the things explicitly listed in that same passage. But many inauguration requirements - like the details of priestly garments - are written in other Torah sections. 'Kachah' cannot reach those.
learning.coreMove: The daf ends without answering. This is an open stub - 'milta' - signaling the discussion continues on the next page. The question is deliberately left dangling to invite the learner to ask: what verse would extend the principle?
learning.resolution: The resolution is left for the next section. The stub question shows that R. Yitzchak bar Bisna's kachah derivation is not complete - it covers only the inauguration text itself. Some further derivation is needed to include outside requirements.

takeaway.text: A word like 'kachah' creates an obligation of exact replication only within the scope of its context. Requirements written elsewhere require their own derivation. The Gemara is transparent about this gap - it ends the page with an open question rather than papering over it.

reasoningPattern.notes: The word 'kachah' (thus/so) in a command signals exact replication of whatever is listed at that point in the text. Requirements written in separate Torah sections are not automatically included under the same kachah.

learning.ahaMoment: R. Yitzchak bar Bisna's answer solves one problem and opens another: 'kachah' finally supplies the missing source for R. Yochanan's whole position, but the word only reaches what is written inside the inauguration passage itself. Requirements written elsewhere in the Torah, like the priestly garments, fall outside its reach, so the daf ends on the very question 'kachah' cannot answer.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think 'kachah' is a blanket rule covering everything in the inauguration. The Gemara limits it precisely: it covers only what is written in the inauguration passage at that point. That limitation creates the stub question that ends the daf.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rivui and meshicha: two more inauguration details that do not invalidate ongoing service. Any combination of seven-day garments and seven-day anointing works. R. Yitzchak bar Bisna finds the source for R. Yochanan's whole position - the word 'kachah' - but then immediately hits the boundary: what about requirements written outside the inauguration text?

argumentFlow.label: Ravina: rivui shiva and meshicha shiva diverge
argumentFlow.speaker: Ravina
argumentFlow.text: Two more practical differences: rivui shiva (wearing the full set of priestly garments on each of the seven inauguration days) and meshicha shiva (anointing on each of the seven days). R. Yochanan holds omitting either invalidates; R. Chanina holds neither does, as neither invalidates ongoing service.

sourceRefs.note: Ravina names rivui shiva and meshicha shiva as the fourth and fifth differences

argumentFlow.label: Lev. 16:32: any combination of anointing and garment-wearing over seven days suffices
argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna (baraita)
argumentFlow.text: Lev. 16:32 says 'the priest who shall be anointed and who shall be consecrated.' Since the YK service already requires a Kohen Gadol, why restate it? The verse teaches: from Exod. 29:30 ('seven days shall the son wear them'), I know only that seven-day garment-wearing AND seven-day anointing validates. What about one without the other? The verse covers all cases: any combination is valid.

sourceRefs.note: Baraita on Lev. 16:32 proving any combination suffices

argumentFlow.label: Source for meshicha requiring seven days ab initio
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: From Exod. 28/29: 'The sacred garments of Aaron shall be for his sons to be anointed in them and consecrated in them.' Anointing is juxtaposed to garment-wearing. Just as garment-wearing must be done for seven days ab initio (from Exod. 29:30), so too anointing must be done for seven days ab initio.

sourceRefs.note: Juxtaposition proof: anointing requires seven days like garment-wearing

argumentFlow.label: R. Yitzchak bar Bisna: 'kachah' is R. Yochanan's source
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yitzchak bar Bisna
argumentFlow.text: The source for R. Yochanan's position: the word 'kachah' (thus/so) in 'do thus to Aaron and his sons' (Lev. 8:34). 'Kachah' means exact replication - every detail written in the inauguration passage must be observed exactly, and failure to do so invalidates.

sourceRefs.note: R. Yitzchak bar Bisna identifies 'kachah' as R. Yochanan's proof-text

argumentFlow.label: Limit of kachah: only covers the inauguration passage
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'Kachah' only covers what is explicitly written in the inauguration context. But many inauguration requirements are written elsewhere in the Torah - priestly garment rules are in Exodus 28, for example. Where do we derive that those extra-textual requirements also invalidate?

sourceRefs.note: Challenge: scope of kachah is limited to the inauguration passage

argumentFlow.label: Open question: milta
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The daf ends with an open stub question - from where do we derive that requirements not written in the inauguration passage but written elsewhere also invalidate? This is left unresolved at the end of 5a.

sourceRefs.note: Stub ending - question continues on next page

conceptRefs.label: Kachah - exact replication required for inauguration
Rivui Begadim - seven days of garment-wearing for KG inauguration

misconceptions.misconception: 'Kachah' creates a blanket rule requiring exact replication of all inauguration conditions, wherever written in the Torah.
misconceptions.correction: 'Kachah' only applies to requirements explicitly written in the inauguration passage at that point. Requirements written in other Torah sections (such as the priestly garment details in Exodus 28) are not automatically covered by this word. The Gemara flags this limitation explicitly.

misconceptions.misconception: For the Kohen Gadol's inauguration to be valid, he must both wear the garments and be anointed on all seven days.
misconceptions.correction: The baraita derives from Lev. 16:32 that any combination of seven days of garment-wearing and seven days of anointing suffices. Even seven days of only one of these without the other is valid. The verse's redundancy teaches 'michal mikol makom' - validity in any case.

visualizableElements.item: Matrix showing three columns (garments x7, anointing x7, both x7) all marked 'valid' based on the baraita's 'michal mikol makom' ruling

Boundary diagram: 'kachah' arrow pointing only at requirements listed inside the inauguration passage (Lev. 8), while requirements from Exod. 28/29 sit outside with a question mark - the stub question made visual

quizSeeds.question: Does the Kohen Gadol need to both wear the priestly garments AND be anointed for all seven inauguration days in order for the inauguration to be valid?
quizSeeds.answer: No. The baraita derived from Lev. 16:32 teaches 'michal mikol makom' - in any case. If he was anointed for seven days but did not wear the garments for seven days, or wore them for seven days without anointing, the inauguration is still valid. Any combination of the two requirements over seven days suffices.

quizSeeds.question: What is R. Yitzchak bar Bisna's source for R. Yochanan's principle that all inauguration requirements are indispensable?
quizSeeds.answer: The word 'kachah' (thus/so) in 'do thus to Aaron and his sons' (Lev. 8:34). 'Kachah' signals exact replication - every detail written in the inauguration passage must be performed exactly, and failure invalidates the service.

quizSeeds.question: Why does the daf end with an open question?
quizSeeds.answer: Because 'kachah' only covers requirements explicitly written in the inauguration passage in Lev. 8. Many inauguration requirements are written elsewhere - for example, the priestly garment details in Exod. 28. 'Kachah' alone cannot make those requirements invalidating conditions. The Gemara ends with a stub ('milta') noting this gap, which the discussion on the next page continues to address.

alternateAngles: The 'any combination suffices' ruling is a classic example of the Gemara using a seemingly redundant verse to expand rather than restrict a rule. A verse that appears to require both anointing and garments actually teaches that either alone suffices. Redundancy in the Torah is always information.

glossary.en: Laying hands on an offering. The worshipper (or Kohen) lays both hands on the head of the animal and leans on it with full weight before it is slaughtered. The Gemara establishes that semikha is a 'shirei mitzva' - a real mitzva component whose omission leaves atonement spiritually incomplete, but which does not invalidate the service.

Waving an offering. Certain offerings or their components are waved before God as part of the ritual. Like semikha, tenufah is a shirei mitzva: the verse seems to attribute atonement to it, but only blood actually atones. Omitting it does not invalidate the service.

A residual or secondary mitzva component - one that complements the core act but does not itself accomplish the primary religious function. If omitted, the Torah treats you as having fallen short of ideal performance, even though the core action (and its legal effect) remain valid. Semikha and tenufah are examples.

Wearing the full set of priestly garments across the seven inauguration days / Anointing with the sacred oil across the seven inauguration days. Both are inauguration requirements that do not invalidate ongoing priestly service. R. Yochanan holds omitting either in the inauguration context invalidates; R. Chanina holds neither does. The baraita teaches that any combination of seven days of garment-wearing or anointing is sufficient to validate the inauguration.

rashiTranslations.en: Something that does not invalidate ongoing service [ladorot]. Below, the Gemara explains this is a 'shirei mitzva' - meaning [omitting it is treated as] if one did not properly perform the mitzva. The phrase 'shirei mitzva' here means a residual component.

He was not careful to treat it as a full mitzva and did not perform semikha: it is as if he did not fully atone. From the choicest [performance]: the verse says 'and he shall lay... and it shall be accepted.'

And he shall atone. Since atonement comes only through blood, as we say in Zevachim (6a): 'he atoned' refers to the man - meaning the essential atonement is in the blood. But he has not achieved full atonement either, since he did not properly fulfill his Master's command.
For the man - the core atonement is through blood. But he also did not atone fully [by not performing the shirei mitzva], since he did not fulfill the command of his Master from the ideal.
From the choicest: the mishna does not teach 'mafrishin' [they separate him as well].
And even though he was not separated, if a disqualifying condition [pesul] occurs
with the first [KG], this second one serves. We derive from this
that prisha [sequestering] does not invalidate [lit. is not an obstacle to] service.

Rivui shiva: that the Kohen Gadol should serve [as KG] for seven consecutive days when he is first appointed to the high priesthood, wearing the eight priestly garments during service. 'Rivui' means multiplying - he multiplies [his use of] garments by serving in all eight while an ordinary kohen serves only in four.

days consecutively when he is appointed to the high priesthood, wearing the eight garments during service. 'Rivui' means multiplying - he is 'multiplied' in garments in that he serves in eight while an ordinary kohen serves in four.
in garments, in that he serves in eight while an ordinary kohen serves in four.
Meshicha shiva: [that they apply] the anointing oil on his head and between his eyebrows for seven consecutive days.
between his eyebrows for seven consecutive days.
[Rashi on 'kol hakatuv bahen']: Everything written [concerning them]: 'seven days shall he fill' [Lev. 8] etc.
And everything that 'filling of hands' [inauguration service] is said there refers to wearing of garments, as it is written [Exod. 29]: 'And the sacred garments which are Aaron's
shall be for his sons after him to be anointed in them' and so on. And anointing
also [requires] all seven [days] because anointing is juxtaposed
to rivui [garment-wearing].
'And the Kohen who is anointed': [This verse is] written in Parshat Acharei Mot, in the context of Yom Kippur.
who is anointed. [It is written] in Parshat Acharei Mot regarding Yom Kippur.
What does Scripture come to teach? If [this is] to teach
that the Yom Kippur service must be performed by a KG - but the entire passage already speaks of Aaron: 'Aaron shall come,' 'Aaron shall give,' 'Aaron shall sacrifice,'
'and Aaron shall come' [Lev. 16]. Because it was said [earlier]:
In Parshat Tetzaveh: 'seven days shall the son who comes after him among his sons who enters the Tent of Meeting on Yom Kippur to serve in the holy place wear them.'

to serve in the holy place. I would only know [he is valid to enter] if he has undergone both rivui shiva and meshicha shiva when appointed. What if [he had] seven [days of garments] and one [day of anointing], or one [day of garments] and seven [days of anointing]? The redundant verse covers all cases: 'in any case.'
one [anointing] and seven [garments] are enough. Hear from this [verse]
I only know [he is valid if he had] both garments and anointing for all seven. The redundant verse teaches: in any case.
only then is he validated. But any combination is valid.
Because it was said [in Parshat Tetzaveh]: 'Seven days shall the son wear them,' and because of this juxtaposition:
in Parshat Tetzaveh: 'seven days shall the son wear them - the Kohen who succeeds him from among his sons who shall come on Yom Kippur into the Tent of Meeting
to serve in the sanctuary.' I only know [he must be anointed]
that he must be qualified to enter the Tent of Meeting on Yom Kippur - meaning only one who has undergone both rivui shiva and meshicha shiva when appointed. Below,
the Gemara asks: where is meshicha shiva written? [Meaning, where does the source for anointing needing seven days appear?]
I only know [he must be qualified]: that is, only one who was anointed for seven days is qualified - and below [the Gemara] asks where meshicha shiva is derived from.
Even one [day of anointing] - do not read this [as] a legitimate textual version.
milta

[Rashi: stub entry.] The daf ends here with the word 'milta,' indicating a question that continues on the following page. R. Yitzchak bar Bisna's 'kachah' derivation covers only what is written in the inauguration passage; requirements written elsewhere still need a separate source.

[Continuation note.] From where do we derive that requirements not written in the inauguration passage itself but written elsewhere in the Torah also invalidate the service if omitted? This question is left open at the end of 5a.
[Note on a non-standard reading.] 'Even one day' - this reading is not in the text. Rashi flags it as a textual variant to be set aside, not a genuine version of the Gemara's discussion.

[Final stub.] The word 'milta' ends the folio. The open question continues on 5b: from where do we derive that inauguration requirements recorded outside the inauguration passage are also indispensable?
